political/territorial_control.label: Territorial Control
political/territorial_control.concept: The percentage of national territory over which the central government exercises effective control — meaning it can enforce laws, collect taxes, and maintain order without significant armed challenge from rival groups or foreign forces.
political/territorial_control.project_transform: Feature vocabulary mapping to score ranges: full_control_no_challenges → 90–100; minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct → 70–89; significant_areas_outside_10_30pct → 50–69; large_portions_contested_30_50pct → 30–49; majority_outside_control → 10–29; failed_state_no_authority → 0–9. Modifiers: foreign_occupation −10, multiple_competing_authorities −15.
political/territorial_control.score_formula:
# Raw input: territory_pct — estimated % of national territory under central govt control (0–100)
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred (qualitative used as fallback)

# Quantitative thresholds (checked in order; first match wins):
if territory_pct >= 95:  score = 90–100
if territory_pct >= 90:  score = 70–89
if territory_pct >= 70:  score = 50–69
if territory_pct >= 50:  score = 30–49
if territory_pct >= 20:  score = 10–29
else:                    score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (fallback; default scores):
full_control_no_challenges          → 90–100  (default 95)
minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct         → 70–89   (default 80)
significant_areas_outside_10_30pct  → 50–69   (default 60)
large_portions_contested_30_50pct   → 30–49   (default 40)
majority_outside_control            → 10–29   (default 20)
failed_state_no_authority           → 0–9     (default 5)
Modifiers: foreign_occupation −10 pts; multiple_competing_authorities −15 pts
Multi-feature rule: minimum (worst feature wins if multiple selected)
caveats: No standardized global index exists for territorial control; all values are qualitative estimates from assessors
Contestation can be continuous (permanent insurgency) or episodic (periodic raids), both affecting scores
Foreign occupation creates ambiguity — the occupying force may 'control' territory but the recognized state does not
Urban vs. rural control often diverges sharply; scores reflect the overall balance
Precision implied by percent scores exceeds actual data quality; treat categories as ranges, not exact values

secondary_sources.name: UN Security Council reports and OCHA situation reports

political/political_violence.label: Political Violence
political/political_violence.concept: The annual total of conflict-related deaths from state-based armed conflict, non-state armed conflict, and one-sided violence (deliberate attacks on civilians). Higher values indicate more deaths; the score is inverted so that higher project scores represent less violence.
political/political_violence.score_formula:
# Raw input: deaths_per_year — annual conflict-related deaths (UCDP GED best_fatality_estimate)
# Input transform: none
# Note: MORE deaths = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted; checked in order):
if deaths_per_year < 100:    score = 90–100
if deaths_per_year < 500:    score = 70–89
if deaths_per_year < 2000:   score = 50–69
if deaths_per_year < 10000:  score = 30–49
if deaths_per_year < 50000:  score = 10–29
else:                        score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
minimal_lt_100         → 90–100  (default 95)
low_100_500            → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_500_2000      → 50–69   (default 60)
high_2000_10000        → 30–49   (default 40)
severe_10000_50000     → 10–29   (default 20)
catastrophic_gt_50000  → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
political/political_violence.coverage_period: 1989–present (GED); ACLED supplements from 1997 for Africa, 2010s globally
political/political_violence.geographic_coverage: Global; UCDP covers all project countries
caveats: UCDP requires a 25-death threshold per conflict-year for inclusion; sub-threshold violence is not counted
UCDP reports three estimates (best, low, high); best estimate is used but may still undercount due to reporting gaps
Active conflict zones with restricted media access are systematically undercounted in both UCDP and ACLED
ACLED (used as supplement) has no minimum threshold and counts non-fatal events; totals from the two sources are not directly comparable
UCDP covers state-based conflict (vs. government), non-state conflict (between armed groups), and one-sided violence (vs. civilians) as three separate datasets that this project aggregates
One-sided state violence (extrajudicial killings, disappearances) is tracked separately in UCDP's one-sided violence dataset and may be undercounted due to information suppression

secondary_sources.name: ACLED (Armed Conflict Location and Event Data Project)

political/institutional_functioning.label: Institutional Functioning
political/institutional_functioning.concept: Whether key state institutions — courts, legislature, bureaucracy, and electoral bodies — operate with meaningful effect. Assesses whether formal institutions exist, are staffed, and produce outputs that shape political outcomes versus existing only on paper.
political/institutional_functioning.series_id: V-Dem liberal democracy index (v2x_libdem) as reference; qualitative feature vocabulary used for scoring
political/institutional_functioning.native_unit: V-Dem 0–1 scale (qualitative feature vocabulary for project scoring)
political/institutional_functioning.native_scale: V-Dem: 0–1 where 1 = full liberal democracy (multiplied by 100 for thresholds)
political/institutional_functioning.project_transform: Feature vocabulary mapping: fully_functional_democratic → 90–100; functional_but_flawed → 70–89; partially_functional → 50–69; severely_degraded → 30–49; collapsed_or_nominal → 10–29; no_functioning_institutions → 0–9.
political/institutional_functioning.score_formula:
# Raw input: vdem_libdem — V-Dem liberal democracy index (v2x_libdem), scale 0–1
# Input transform: none (multiply by 100 as presentation step before threshold lookup)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

vdem_scaled = vdem_libdem * 100  # → 0–100

# Quantitative thresholds:
if vdem_scaled >= 80:  score = 90–100
if vdem_scaled >= 60:  score = 70–89
if vdem_scaled >= 40:  score = 50–69
if vdem_scaled >= 20:  score = 30–49
if vdem_scaled >= 5:   score = 10–29
else:                  score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (fallback):
fully_functional_democratic   → 90–100  (default 95)
functional_but_flawed         → 70–89   (default 80)
partially_functional          → 50–69   (default 55)
severely_degraded             → 30–49   (default 40)
collapsed_or_nominal          → 10–29   (default 20)
no_functioning_institutions   → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
political/institutional_functioning.coverage_period: 1789–present (V-Dem); qualitative for all 39 countries from their start year
political/institutional_functioning.geographic_coverage: V-Dem covers 180+ countries; qualitative assessment fills gaps
caveats: No single V-Dem index maps cleanly to 'institutional functioning' — assessors use multiple indices as cross-checks
Freedom House 'Political Rights' and governance sub-ratings supplement V-Dem for consistency checks
Transitional periods often show institutions technically operating while controlled by a single faction — scoring requires distinguishing nominal from substantive functioning
V-Dem uses 'measurement model' (Bayesian aggregation of expert surveys) that introduces uncertainty estimates; point estimates are used here
Coup periods and transitional governments create ambiguity about which institution is the 'legitimate' one to assess

secondary_sources.name: Freedom House Freedom in the World — Political Rights subcategories

political/civil_liberties.label: Civil Liberties
political/civil_liberties.concept: The degree to which individuals enjoy freedoms of expression, association, religion, and movement, and are protected from arbitrary state action. Measured through Freedom House's annual assessment.
political/civil_liberties.primary_source: Freedom House — Freedom in the World
political/civil_liberties.native_unit: Ordinal rating 1–7 (1 = most free, 7 = least free)
political/civil_liberties.native_scale: 1–7 rating, derived from 15 questions across four subcategories (D: Expression and Belief; E: Associational Rights; F: Rule of Law; G: Personal Autonomy), each question scored 0–4, summed to 0–60 raw score, then binned to 1–7.
political/civil_liberties.score_formula:
# Raw input: FH_CL — Freedom House Civil Liberties rating, 1–7 (1 = most free, 7 = least free)
# Input transform: freedom_house_invert — converts 1–7 (1=best) to 0–100 (100=best)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

transformed = (8 - FH_CL) / 7 * 100
#   FH_CL = 1  →  transformed = 100.0  (most free)
#   FH_CL = 2  →  transformed =  85.7
#   FH_CL = 3  →  transformed =  71.4
#   FH_CL = 4  →  transformed =  57.1
#   FH_CL = 5  →  transformed =  42.9
#   FH_CL = 6  →  transformed =  28.6
#   FH_CL = 7  →  transformed =  14.3  (least free)

# Threshold scoring after transform:
if transformed >= 85:  score = 90–100
if transformed >= 70:  score = 70–89
if transformed >= 50:  score = 50–69
if transformed >= 30:  score = 30–49
if transformed >= 15:  score = 10–29
else:                  score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (fallback):
full_freedoms                   → 90–100  (default 95)
mostly_free_minor_restrictions  → 70–89   (default 80)
partially_free                  → 50–69   (default 57)
significant_restrictions        → 30–49   (default 43)
severe_restrictions             → 10–29   (default 20)
no_civil_liberties              → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
political/civil_liberties.methodology_break: Methodology revisions occurred in 2003 and again around 2018–2020 (restructuring of questions). Freedom House states scores are not revised retroactively; changes at methodological transition points may reflect instrument changes, not actual conditions.
caveats: Scores reflect assessor judgment; Freedom House acknowledges 'an element of subjectivity is unavoidable'
The 1–7 rating collapses a 0–60 raw score into seven bins; countries near bin boundaries can shift rating with minimal real change
Systematic annual coverage begins 1973; coverage before that date is not comparable
1981 is skipped — the survey covering January 1981 to August 1982 is assigned to 1982
No retroactive corrections — methodology changes affect forward comparability, not historical records

secondary_sources.name: US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights Practices

political/elite_cohesion.label: Elite Cohesion
political/elite_cohesion.concept: Whether the ruling coalition is stable and internally cohesive, or whether it is fragmenting through defections, factional conflict, or coup attempts. Assesses the distribution of power among elites and the risk of leadership instability.
political/elite_cohesion.primary_source: Qualitative assessment (ICG reports, academic literature, news analysis)
political/elite_cohesion.project_transform: Feature vocabulary mapping: stable_unified_governance → 90–100; minor_tensions_functional → 70–89; significant_competition_managed → 50–69; open_factional_conflict → 30–49; active_power_struggles → 10–29; complete_fragmentation → 0–9.
political/elite_cohesion.score_formula:
# Raw input: qualitative assessment only — no standard quantitative index exists
# Combination rule: qualitative_only

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (default scores):
stable_unified_governance        → 90–100  (default 95)
minor_tensions_functional        → 70–89   (default 80)
significant_competition_managed  → 50–69   (default 60)
open_factional_conflict          → 30–49   (default 40)
active_power_struggles           → 10–29   (default 20)
complete_fragmentation           → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum (worst feature wins)
caveats: No standardized global index measures elite cohesion directly; all values are qualitative assessor judgments
V-Dem 'polyarchy' and 'party system institutionalization' indices provide cross-checks but do not directly measure elite cohesion
Indicator is most consequential for non-democratic regimes where palace politics are opaque
Sources often only reveal elite fractures after the fact (coups, defections), creating a recency bias in assessments

secondary_sources.name: V-Dem Party Institutionalization and Executive Constraints Indices
Archigos Leadership Dataset (coup events and leadership changes)
International Crisis Group — Elite Politics Reports

political/military_expenditure.label: Military Expenditure
political/military_expenditure.concept: Government military spending as a share of GDP. Elevated spending signals a war economy or security-state priorities at the expense of social and productive investment. The score is inverted: lower spending earns a higher score.
political/military_expenditure.series_id: SIPRI share of GDP series; World Bank MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS as supplement
political/military_expenditure.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower spending = higher score): < 1% → 90–100; < 2% → 70–89; < 4% → 50–69; < 7% → 30–49; < 15% → 10–29; ≥ 15% → 0–9.
political/military_expenditure.score_formula:
# Raw input: milex_pct_gdp — military expenditure as % of GDP (SIPRI % GDP series)
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER spending = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if milex_pct_gdp < 1:   score = 90–100
if milex_pct_gdp < 2:   score = 70–89
if milex_pct_gdp < 4:   score = 50–69
if milex_pct_gdp < 7:   score = 30–49
if milex_pct_gdp < 15:  score = 10–29
else:                   score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
minimal_military_spending_lt_1pct → 90–100  (default 95)
low_1_2pct                        → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_2_4pct                   → 50–69   (default 60)
elevated_4_7pct                   → 30–49   (default 40)
high_7_15pct                      → 10–29   (default 20)
war_economy_gt_15pct              → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
political/military_expenditure.coverage_period: 1988–present (SIPRI); World Bank WDI from 1960 for some countries
caveats: SIPRI figures represent the best estimate from primary and secondary sources; data quality varies, especially for conflict-affected states where budgets are opaque
Off-budget military spending (paramilitary forces, intelligence agencies, debt servicing for past arms purchases) is not captured
NATO reporting convention includes military R&D and military pensions; many non-NATO countries use narrower definitions
Very low figures (< 0.5%) may reflect data gaps rather than genuinely minimal spending
High spending can reflect genuine wartime necessity or peacetime militarism; the indicator does not distinguish between these

political/political_stability.label: Political Stability
political/political_stability.concept: Perceptions of the likelihood that the government will be destabilized or overthrown by unconstitutional or violent means, including politically motivated violence and terrorism. Higher values indicate a more stable political environment.
political/political_stability.series_id: PV.EST (Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism)
political/political_stability.native_scale: Standard normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; typical range −2.5 to +2.5. Derived from ~30 underlying data sources aggregated via Bayesian Unobserved Components Model (UCM). Higher = more stable.
political/political_stability.score_formula:
# Raw input: WGI_PV — World Bank WGI Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism
# Series: PV.EST, standard normal distribution (z-score), typical range −2.5 to +2.5
# Input transform: none (WGI scale used directly)
# Combination rule: qualitative_preferred (qualitative assessment preferred; WGI as fallback)

# Quantitative thresholds:
if WGI_PV >= 1.5:   score = 90–100
if WGI_PV >= 0.5:   score = 75–89
if WGI_PV >= -0.5:  score = 55–74
if WGI_PV >= -1.5:  score = 35–54
if WGI_PV >= -2.0:  score = 15–34
else:               score = 0–14

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (preferred):
very_stable                   → 90–100  (default 95)
stable                        → 75–89   (default 82)
mild_instability              → 55–74   (default 65)
significant_instability       → 35–54   (default 45)
high_instability              → 15–34   (default 25)
extreme_instability_conflict  → 0–14    (default 7)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
political/political_stability.methodology_break: WGI methodology was updated in 2011 (revised UCM approach); pre-2011 and post-2011 estimates are broadly comparable but small discontinuities exist at the revision boundary.
caveats: WGI aggregates perception-based surveys (EIU, ICRG, PRS, Freedom House, Bertelsmann, etc.); the composite estimate masks disagreement between underlying sources
Standard errors are substantial (95% CI can span ±0.5–1.0 units); year-to-year changes smaller than 0.3 standard deviations should not be treated as meaningful
PV.EST conflates two conceptually distinct phenomena: coup risk (elite stability) and terrorist/insurgent violence (security environment)
Coverage of small states and territories may rely on fewer underlying sources, widening uncertainty

political/government_effectiveness.label: Gov't Effectiveness
political/government_effectiveness.concept: Perceptions of the quality of public services, the quality of the civil service and the degree of its independence from political pressures, the quality of policy formulation and implementation, and the credibility of the government's commitment to such policies.
political/government_effectiveness.native_scale: Standard normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; typical range −2.5 to +2.5. Derived from ~30 underlying data sources aggregated via Bayesian UCM. Higher = more effective government.
political/government_effectiveness.project_transform: Threshold scoring (WGI GE.EST scale, no transform required): ≥ 1.5 → 85–100; ≥ 0.5 → 70–84; ≥ −0.5 → 50–69; ≥ −1.5 → 25–49; < −1.5 → 0–24.
political/government_effectiveness.score_formula:
# Raw input: WGI_GE — World Bank WGI Government Effectiveness
# Series: GE.EST, standard normal distribution (z-score), typical range −2.5 to +2.5
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: qualitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if WGI_GE >= 1.5:   score = 85–100
if WGI_GE >= 0.5:   score = 70–84
if WGI_GE >= -0.5:  score = 50–69
if WGI_GE >= -1.5:  score = 25–49
else:               score = 0–24

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (preferred):
very_effective           → 85–100  (default 92)
effective                → 70–84   (default 77)
partially_effective      → 50–69   (default 60)
low_effectiveness        → 25–49   (default 37)
ineffective_or_captured  → 0–24    (default 12)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: GE.EST relies heavily on business-environment perception surveys (EIU, ICRG, World Bank Enterprise Survey) that may overweight formal sector experience
During active conflict, government effectiveness collapses but the WGI may lag by 1–2 years as underlying survey data takes time to reflect deterioration
Combines very different concepts: public service delivery quality, bureaucratic independence, and policy implementation credibility

economic/gdp_per_capita.label: GDP per Capita
economic/gdp_per_capita.concept: The value of all goods and services produced per person in a country in a given year. Used as a proxy for living standards. The project indexes each country's GDP per capita to its own historical peak, so the score reflects how far the country is from its best observed performance.
economic/gdp_per_capita.series_id: NY.GDP.PCAP.CD (current USD); NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD (PPP, constant 2017 international $) as supplement
economic/gdp_per_capita.native_scale: Raw USD values (no inherent upper bound)
economic/gdp_per_capita.project_transform: (value / peak_value_in_series) × 100, where peak_value is the highest observed GDP per capita for that country across the full data period. Resulting score: 0–100 where 100 = peak year, scores > 100 impossible.
economic/gdp_per_capita.score_formula:
# Raw input: gdp_per_capita_usd — current USD per person (World Bank NY.GDP.PCAP.CD)
# Input transform: percent_of_peak — indexes each country to its own historical maximum
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

peak = max(gdp_per_capita_usd for all years in this country's data series)
transformed = gdp_per_capita_usd / peak * 100  # → 0–100 (100 = peak year; cannot exceed 100)

# Threshold scoring after transform:
if transformed >= 95:  score = 90–100
if transformed >= 80:  score = 70–89
if transformed >= 60:  score = 50–69
if transformed >= 40:  score = 30–49
if transformed >= 20:  score = 10–29
else:                  score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (fallback):
above_peak                    → 90–100  (default 95)
near_peak_90_100pct           → 70–89   (default 85)
moderate_decline_70_90pct     → 50–69   (default 65)
significant_decline_50_70pct  → 30–49   (default 45)
severe_decline_30_50pct       → 10–29   (default 25)
collapse_below_30pct          → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Current USD values (NY.GDP.PCAP.CD) are sensitive to exchange rate fluctuations; a devaluation can cause apparent GDP collapse even without real economic decline
PPP-adjusted values (NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD) are more stable but rely on IMF price comparison surveys that are updated irregularly (2005, 2011, 2017 ICP rounds)
Indexing to country peak means the score is relative to that country's history, not cross-country comparable — a score of 50 means very different absolute income levels in different countries
Conflict and sanctions can disrupt national accounts compilation; World Bank may use IMF or UN estimates that lag by 1–3 years for affected countries
Informal economy activity (significant in conflict-affected states) is often underestimated in national accounts

secondary_sources.name: IMF World Economic Outlook — NGDPDPC and PPPPC series
UN Statistics Division National Accounts Data
Maddison Project Database (historical GDP back to 1820)

economic/inflation.label: Inflation
economic/inflation.concept: The annual percentage change in consumer prices. Low, stable inflation indicates a functioning monetary system; high inflation (especially hyperinflation) reflects monetary collapse and erodes real incomes. The score is inverted: lower inflation earns a higher score.
economic/inflation.native_scale: Annual % change; can exceed 1,000% or more in hyperinflation
economic/inflation.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower inflation = higher score): < 3% → 90–100; < 10% → 70–89; < 25% → 50–69; < 50% → 30–49; < 100% → 10–29; ≥ 100% → 0–9.
economic/inflation.score_formula:
# Raw input: inflation_pct — annual % change in consumer prices (World Bank FP.CPI.TOTL.ZG)
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER inflation = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted; checked in order):
if inflation_pct < 3:    score = 90–100
if inflation_pct < 10:   score = 70–89
if inflation_pct < 25:   score = 50–69
if inflation_pct < 50:   score = 30–49
if inflation_pct < 100:  score = 10–29
else:                    score = 0–9
# Note: deflation (negative CPI) treated same as 0–3% → scores 90–100

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_3pct              → 90–100  (default 95)
moderate_3_10pct         → 70–89   (default 80)
elevated_10_25pct        → 50–69   (default 60)
high_25_50pct            → 30–49   (default 40)
very_high_50_100pct      → 10–29   (default 20)
hyperinflation_gt_100pct → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: The World Bank series uses each country's own national CPI, which may cover only urban areas (many developing countries use urban CPI as national proxy)
Hyperinflation (>100%) is poorly differentiated at extremes — 200% and 10,000% both score near 0; for hyperinflation analysis, use IMF or central bank data directly
Conflict disrupts price data collection; inflation figures for active war years may be estimates with wide uncertainty
Deflation (negative values) is scored as near-perfect (same bin as 0–3%), which may be inappropriate if caused by demand collapse
IMF data is used as fallback when World Bank is absent; the two series can diverge for crisis years due to different estimation methods

secondary_sources.name: IMF International Financial Statistics — CPI (IFS line 64)
IMF World Economic Outlook — PCPIPCH series
World Bank Global Economic Monitor (GEM) commodity prices

economic/unemployment.label: Unemployment
economic/unemployment.concept: The percentage of the labor force that is without work, currently available for work, and actively seeking employment. The score is inverted: lower unemployment earns a higher score.
economic/unemployment.series_id: SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS (unemployment, total, % of total labor force, ILO modeled estimates)
economic/unemployment.numerator: Persons without employment, available for work, actively seeking work
economic/unemployment.denominator: Total labor force (employed + unemployed; excludes discouraged workers and those outside labor force)
economic/unemployment.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower unemployment = higher score): < 5% → 90–100; < 10% → 70–89; < 15% → 50–69; < 25% → 30–49; < 40% → 10–29; ≥ 40% → 0–9.
economic/unemployment.score_formula:
# Raw input: unemployment_pct — % of total labor force (ILO modeled estimates, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS)
# ILO definition: not employed, currently available for work, and actively seeking work
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER unemployment = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if unemployment_pct < 5:   score = 90–100
if unemployment_pct < 10:  score = 70–89
if unemployment_pct < 15:  score = 50–69
if unemployment_pct < 25:  score = 30–49
if unemployment_pct < 40:  score = 10–29
else:                      score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_5pct        → 90–100  (default 95)
moderate_5_10pct   → 70–89   (default 80)
elevated_10_15pct  → 50–69   (default 60)
high_15_25pct      → 30–49   (default 40)
very_high_25_40pct → 10–29   (default 20)
crisis_gt_40pct    → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
economic/unemployment.geographic_coverage: 189 countries (modeled estimates; some gaps for conflict-affected states)
economic/unemployment.methodology_break: ILO revised its modeled estimation methodology in 2017 (ILO Modelled Estimates, ILOSTAT) and again in 2020. Historical series may have been revised; use caution for trends spanning revision points.
caveats: The ILO definition requires three simultaneous conditions: not employed, currently available, and actively seeking work — this excludes discouraged workers who have given up searching
Informal sector workers who have some income-generating activity count as 'employed' even if working one hour per week (ILO threshold)
National labor force surveys vary in design, frequency, and coverage; World Bank uses ILO modeled estimates to fill gaps, introducing additional uncertainty
For conflict-affected or fragile states, unemployment data may not exist; ILO modeled estimates carry wider uncertainty bands
Underemployment (working below capacity or skills) is not captured
Youth unemployment often runs 2–3× the overall rate; the aggregate figure can mask severe youth labor market stress

secondary_sources.name: ILO ILOSTAT — Unemployment Rate by Sex and Age
IMF World Economic Outlook — LUR (unemployment rate)

economic/trade_openness.label: Trade Openness
economic/trade_openness.concept: The sum of exports and imports of goods and services as a percentage of GDP. A standard measure of economic integration with the world economy. Higher values indicate more trade relative to the size of the economy.
economic/trade_openness.numerator: Exports of goods and services + Imports of goods and services (current USD)
economic/trade_openness.score_formula:
# Raw input: trade_pct_gdp — (exports + imports of goods & services) / GDP × 100 (NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS)
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if trade_pct_gdp >= 100:  score = 90–100
if trade_pct_gdp >= 70:   score = 70–89
if trade_pct_gdp >= 40:   score = 50–69
if trade_pct_gdp >= 20:   score = 30–49
if trade_pct_gdp >= 10:   score = 10–29
else:                     score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
highly_open_gt_100pct  → 90–100  (default 95)
open_70_100pct         → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_40_70pct      → 50–69   (default 60)
restricted_20_40pct    → 30–49   (default 40)
closed_lt_20pct        → 10–29   (default 20)
autarky_or_blockade    → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Includes BOTH goods AND services (not just merchandise trade); services can be significant for tourism-dependent economies
Small transit economies (e.g., Singapore, Timor-Leste) show trade ratios far exceeding 100% due to re-exports; this inflates openness scores without indicating deeper economic integration
Sanctions-affected countries may have severely suppressed trade not fully captured in official statistics (smuggling and sanctions-busting create shadow trade)
Conflict disrupts trade data collection; figures for active war years may be rough estimates
Does not distinguish between voluntary trade policy openness and exogenous trade collapse due to conflict or sanctions

economic/fiscal_health.label: Fiscal Health
economic/fiscal_health.concept: The sustainability of government finances, measured primarily by gross government debt as a percentage of GDP. Lower debt relative to GDP indicates more fiscal space and lower risk of debt distress. The score is inverted: lower debt earns a higher score.
economic/fiscal_health.series_id: GGXWDG_NGDP (general government gross debt as % of GDP)
economic/fiscal_health.native_scale: 0–200%+ (no theoretical upper bound; hyperinflation countries can show extreme values)
economic/fiscal_health.score_formula:
# Raw input: debt_pct_gdp — general govt gross debt as % of GDP (IMF WEO: GGXWDG_NGDP)
# Definition: outstanding stock of all government liabilities; gross (not net of assets)
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER debt = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if debt_pct_gdp < 30:   score = 90–100
if debt_pct_gdp < 60:   score = 70–89
if debt_pct_gdp < 90:   score = 50–69
if debt_pct_gdp < 120:  score = 30–49
if debt_pct_gdp < 200:  score = 10–29
else:                   score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
surplus_low_debt_lt_30pct           → 90–100  (default 95)
small_deficit_manageable_30_60pct   → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_deficit_elevated_60_90pct  → 50–69   (default 60)
large_deficit_high_90_120pct        → 30–49   (default 40)
unsustainable_debt_crisis           → 10–29   (default 20)
fiscal_collapse                     → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
economic/fiscal_health.coverage_period: 1980–present (most countries); earlier for some; IMF WEO published twice yearly
economic/fiscal_health.frequency: Annual (IMF WEO spring and fall editions)
caveats: Scope is 'general government' = central government + state/regional/local governments + social security funds; excludes state-owned enterprises, central bank quasi-fiscal activities
Gross debt (not net): does not subtract government financial assets (deposits, loans receivable); a country with large assets and large liabilities appears high-debt but may not be
Central government vs. general government debt can differ by 10–20 pp of GDP depending on structure of sub-national borrowing
Off-balance-sheet liabilities (PPP guarantees, contingent liabilities, implicit pension obligations) are excluded
Conflict-affected states often cannot compile debt statistics; IMF may not publish figures, creating gaps in series
High debt in a country with its own currency and central bank differs fundamentally from high debt in a dollarized economy — the score does not distinguish these

economic/natural_resource_rents.label: Resource Rents
economic/natural_resource_rents.concept: Total natural resources rents as a percentage of GDP — the economic surplus above extraction costs from oil, gas, coal, minerals, and forests. High resource dependence is associated with the 'resource curse': weaker institutions, lower economic diversification, and increased conflict risk. The score is inverted and capped at 84, reflecting a permanent penalty for high resource dependence.
economic/natural_resource_rents.series_id: NY.GDP.TOTL.RT.ZS (total natural resources rents, % of GDP)
economic/natural_resource_rents.numerator: Total economic rent from oil + natural gas + coal + mineral + forest resources
economic/natural_resource_rents.native_scale: 0–80%+ (Gulf states have historically exceeded 50%; most countries < 10%)
economic/natural_resource_rents.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower rents = higher score); maximum score is 84 (not 100) to reflect structural resource-dependence penalty: < 5% → 70–84; < 15% → 50–64; < 30% → 30–49; < 50% → 15–29; < 65% → 5–14; ≥ 65% → 0–9.
economic/natural_resource_rents.score_formula:
# Raw input: rents_pct_gdp — total natural resources rents as % of GDP (NY.GDP.TOTL.RT.ZS)
# World Bank definition: rents = unit resource price minus average extraction costs
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER rents = LOWER score (inverted; resource-curse framing)
# Note: MAXIMUM score is 84, not 100 — intentional ceiling for resource-dependent economies
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted; max score 84):
if rents_pct_gdp < 5:   score = 70–84
if rents_pct_gdp < 15:  score = 50–64
if rents_pct_gdp < 30:  score = 30–49
if rents_pct_gdp < 50:  score = 15–29
if rents_pct_gdp < 65:  score = 5–14
else:                   score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
minimal_lt_5pct          → 70–84  (default 77)
moderate_5_15pct         → 50–64  (default 57)
significant_15_30pct     → 30–49  (default 40)
high_30_50pct            → 15–29  (default 22)
very_high_40_60pct       → 5–14   (default 10)
extremely_high_gt_60pct  → 0–9    (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Rent calculations depend on world commodity price estimates applied to extraction cost assumptions; price volatility creates year-to-year score fluctuations unrelated to actual governance changes
Resource curse literature shows non-linear effects: moderate rents can fund development while very high rents crowd out diversification — the scoring reflects this with a non-linear threshold table
Maximum score of 84 reflects that no resource-dependent economy has fully escaped the structural risks associated with resource wealth
Forest rents are included but poorly measured in conflict-affected states where illegal logging is prevalent
Gas flaring and stranded resources may reduce effective rent capture below the theoretical value

secondary_sources.name: IMF Fiscal Affairs Department — Fiscal Policy and Resource Wealth

economic/youth_unemployment.label: Youth Unemployment
economic/youth_unemployment.concept: The unemployment rate among persons aged 15–24, as a percentage of the youth labor force. Youth unemployment is a sensitive indicator of social and economic dysfunction, often running 2–3 times the overall rate. The score is inverted: lower youth unemployment earns a higher score.
economic/youth_unemployment.series_id: SL.UEM.1524.ZS (unemployment, youth total, % of total labor force ages 15–24, ILO modeled estimates)
economic/youth_unemployment.numerator: Youth (15–24) without employment, available for work, actively seeking work
economic/youth_unemployment.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower youth unemployment = higher score): < 10% → 80–100; < 20% → 55–79; < 30% → 30–54; < 40% → 10–29; ≥ 40% → 0–9.
economic/youth_unemployment.score_formula:
# Raw input: youth_unemp_pct — youth unemployment rate, ages 15–24 (SL.UEM.1524.ZS)
# ILO definition: not employed, available for work, actively seeking, aged 15–24
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER youth unemployment = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if youth_unemp_pct < 10:  score = 80–100
if youth_unemp_pct < 20:  score = 55–79
if youth_unemp_pct < 30:  score = 30–54
if youth_unemp_pct < 40:  score = 10–29
else:                     score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_10pct        → 80–100  (default 90)
moderate_10_20pct   → 55–79   (default 67)
elevated_20_30pct   → 30–54   (default 42)
high_30_40pct       → 10–29   (default 20)
very_high_gt_40pct  → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
economic/youth_unemployment.geographic_coverage: 189 countries (modeled; gaps for some conflict-affected states)
caveats: Youth unemployment runs 2–3× the overall rate in most countries; high youth unemployment can coexist with moderate overall unemployment
ILO modeled estimates fill gaps in national labor force survey coverage; uncertainty is higher for conflict-affected and fragile states
Discouraged workers (youth who have given up seeking work) are excluded from the unemployment rate, causing under-measurement in crisis contexts
Youth NEET rate (not in employment, education, or training) is a broader indicator but less consistently available; not used here

secondary_sources.name: ILO ILOSTAT — Youth Labour Market Indicators

economic/gini.label: Inequality (Gini)
economic/gini.concept: The Gini coefficient measures income or consumption inequality within a country on a 0–100 scale: 0 = perfect equality (all incomes identical); 100 = perfect inequality (one person holds all income). The score is inverted: lower inequality earns a higher score.
economic/gini.native_scale: 0–100 where 0 = perfect equality; typical range 25–65 in practice. Derived from household income or consumption surveys; exact definition varies (pre-tax income, post-transfer income, or consumption expenditure).
economic/gini.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower inequality = higher score): < 30 → 75–100; < 40 → 50–74; < 50 → 25–49; ≥ 50 → 0–24.
economic/gini.score_formula:
# Raw input: gini_coeff — Gini coefficient (World Bank SI.POV.GINI, scale 0–100)
# Definition: 0 = perfect equality; 100 = one person holds all income
# Input transform: none
# Note: LOWER gini = LESS inequality = HIGHER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if gini_coeff < 30:  score = 75–100
if gini_coeff < 40:  score = 50–74
if gini_coeff < 50:  score = 25–49
else:                score = 0–24

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_30       → 75–100  (default 87)
moderate_30_40  → 50–74   (default 62)
elevated_40_50  → 25–49   (default 37)
high_gt_50      → 0–24    (default 12)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
economic/gini.frequency: Irregular (household surveys; typically every 3–5 years per country)
caveats: Gini is survey-based and not collected annually; most countries have surveys every 3–5 years; values between surveys require interpolation
Income vs. consumption Gini can differ by 5–10 points; the World Bank series mixes both depending on country; direct cross-country comparison requires caution
Post-conflict and crisis years often have no survey data; the Gini series may not capture peak inequality episodes
Top-income underreporting in surveys causes all household surveys to underestimate true inequality; the Gini understates inequality for high-income countries with large capital income
SWIID (Standardized World Income Inequality Database) harmonizes Gini estimates across concepts; used as cross-check but not primary source

secondary_sources.name: SWIID — Standardized World Income Inequality Database

economic/neet_rate.label: NEET Rate
economic/neet_rate.concept: The percentage of youth aged 15–24 who are Not in Education, Employment, or Training (NEET). A broader measure of youth marginalisation than unemployment alone: it captures discouraged workers and those outside the formal labour market entirely. Higher NEET rates signal greater social and economic dysfunction; the score is inverted.
economic/neet_rate.series_id: SL.UEM.NEET.ZS (% of youth not in education, employment or training, total)
economic/neet_rate.numerator: Youth aged 15–24 neither employed nor in education or training
economic/neet_rate.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower NEET = higher score): < 10% → 80–100; < 20% → 55–79; < 30% → 30–54; < 40% → 10–29; ≥ 40% → 0–9.
economic/neet_rate.score_formula:
# Raw input: neet_pct — % youth 15–24 not in education/employment/training
# ILO/World Bank definition: excludes those in school or employed
# Input transform: none (inverted scale)
# Note: HIGHER NEET = LOWER score
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

if neet_pct < 10:  score = 80–100
if neet_pct < 20:  score = 55–79
if neet_pct < 30:  score = 30–54
if neet_pct < 40:  score = 10–29
else:              score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_10pct        → 80–100  (default 90)
moderate_10_20pct   → 55–79   (default 67)
elevated_20_30pct   → 30–54   (default 42)
high_30_40pct       → 10–29   (default 20)
very_high_gt_40pct  → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: NEET availability is patchier than youth unemployment for many conflict-affected and fragile states; values before ~2000 are rarely available
Definitions vary: some countries include 15–29 age band; series uses the ILO standard 15–24 band where available
In high-fertility settings, many young women are outside the labour force by choice (family care); NEET rates can appear high without implying economic crisis

economic/poverty_rate.label: Poverty Rate
economic/poverty_rate.concept: The share of the population living below the international poverty line of $2.15 per person per day (2017 PPP). A direct measure of extreme material deprivation. Higher poverty rates signal worse economic outcomes; the score is inverted.
economic/poverty_rate.primary_source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform
economic/poverty_rate.series_id: SI.POV.DDAY (poverty headcount ratio at $2.15 a day, 2017 PPP, % of population)
economic/poverty_rate.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower poverty rate = higher score): < 3% → 80–100; < 10% → 60–79; < 25% → 35–59; < 50% → 10–34; ≥ 50% → 0–9.
economic/poverty_rate.score_formula:
# Raw input: poverty_pct — % population below $2.15/day (2017 PPP)
# World Bank PIP definition: international absolute poverty line
# Input transform: none (inverted scale)
# Note: HIGHER poverty = LOWER score
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

if poverty_pct < 3:   score = 80–100
if poverty_pct < 10:  score = 60–79
if poverty_pct < 25:  score = 35–59
if poverty_pct < 50:  score = 10–34
else:                 score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
very_low_lt_3pct      → 80–100  (default 90)
low_3_10pct           → 60–79   (default 70)
moderate_10_25pct     → 35–59   (default 47)
high_25_50pct         → 10–34   (default 22)
severe_gt_50pct       → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
economic/poverty_rate.methodology_break: World Bank updated the international poverty line from $1.90 (2011 PPP) to $2.15 (2017 PPP) in September 2022. Historical series have been revised back consistently by the Bank; comparability is maintained.
caveats: Survey-based; annual data are modeled interpolations between surveys, not direct observations
Conflict years often have no reliable household surveys; poverty estimates for fragile states carry high uncertainty
The $2.15/day line captures extreme poverty; near-poverty and vulnerability are not reflected in this series
In-kind transfers, subsistence agriculture, and informal support networks reduce measured poverty below true welfare levels

secondary_sources.name: UNDP Human Development Reports — Multidimensional Poverty Index

economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.label: Inequality-Adjusted Income
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.concept: GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD) adjusted downward by the country's Gini coefficient to reflect the income level of a typical person rather than the mean. Computed as: GDP_pc × (1 − Gini/100). Captures both the average income level and its distribution in a single metric. Like gdp_per_capita, scored as a percentage of the country's own peak value (percent_of_peak transform).
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.primary_source: World Bank WDI (GDP per capita) + Gini series
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.numerator: GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD) × (1 − Gini coefficient/100)
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.native_scale: Indexed to country historical peak (0–100 after transform)
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.project_transform: percent_of_peak: (value / max(series)) × 100. Same thresholds as GDP per capita: ≥ 95% → 90–100; ≥ 80% → 70–89; ≥ 60% → 50–69; ≥ 40% → 25–49; ≥ 20% → 10–24; < 20% → 0–9.
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita.score_formula:
# Raw input: gini_adj_gdp — GDP_pc × (1 − Gini/100) in constant 2015 USD
# World Bank NY.GDP.PCAP.KD × (1 − SI.POV.GINI/100)
# Input transform: percent_of_peak — indexed to country's own historical max
# Note: Gini series has gaps; years with missing Gini use linear interpolation
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

transformed = (gini_adj_gdp / peak_gini_adj_gdp) * 100
if transformed >= 95:  score = 90–100
if transformed >= 80:  score = 70–89
if transformed >= 60:  score = 50–69
if transformed >= 40:  score = 25–49
if transformed >= 20:  score = 10–24
else:                  score = 0–9
caveats: Gini series has substantial gaps; years without survey data require interpolation, introducing measurement uncertainty
The adjustment penalises unequal countries but does not capture non-income welfare dimensions
GDP per capita itself does not capture home production, informal economy, or in-kind transfers

economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.label: Inequality-Adjusted Income (PPP)
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.concept: Same as gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita but using GDP per capita in constant 2017 international PPP dollars (NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD), enabling cross-country comparison on a purchasing-power-equivalent basis. Scored as percent_of_peak relative to each country's own maximum.
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.primary_source: World Bank WDI (GDP per capita PPP) + Gini series
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.numerator: GDP per capita (constant 2017 PPP $) × (1 − Gini coefficient/100)
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.native_scale: Indexed to country historical peak (0–100 after transform)
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.project_transform: percent_of_peak: (value / max(series)) × 100. Same thresholds as GDP per capita: ≥ 95% → 90–100; ≥ 80% → 70–89; ≥ 60% → 50–69; ≥ 40% → 25–49; ≥ 20% → 10–24; < 20% → 0–9.
economic/gini_adjusted_gdp_per_capita_pp.score_formula:
# Raw input: gini_adj_gdp_ppp — GDP_pc_PPP × (1 − Gini/100) in 2017 PPP $
# World Bank NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD × (1 − SI.POV.GINI/100)
# Input transform: percent_of_peak
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

transformed = (gini_adj_gdp_ppp / peak_gini_adj_gdp_ppp) * 100
if transformed >= 95:  score = 90–100
if transformed >= 80:  score = 70–89
if transformed >= 60:  score = 50–69
if transformed >= 40:  score = 25–49
if transformed >= 20:  score = 10–24
else:                  score = 0–9
caveats: PPP conversion factors change with each ICP round (2005, 2011, 2017); historical series revised retroactively
Gini availability limits the series to years with interpolated or survey-based estimates

economic/export_diversification.label: Export Diversification
economic/export_diversification.concept: The degree to which a country's export basket is diversified across product categories and trading partners. Higher diversification (lower dependence on a single commodity or market) is associated with greater economic resilience. Measured via the inverse of the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) or the IMF Export Diversification Index; higher score = more diversified.
economic/export_diversification.series_id: Derived from UNCTAD bilateral trade data or World Bank WITS HS commodity classifications
economic/export_diversification.native_unit: Index (0–1 where 1 = fully diversified; or inverse HHI)
economic/export_diversification.project_transform: Direct threshold scoring (higher diversification = higher score): > 0.8 → 75–100; > 0.6 → 55–74; > 0.4 → 30–54; > 0.2 → 10–29; ≤ 0.2 → 0–9.
economic/export_diversification.score_formula:
# Raw input: export_div — diversification index (0=concentrated, 1=diversified)
# Normalised inverse Herfindahl-Hirschman Index or equivalent
# Input transform: none (direct scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

if export_div > 0.8:  score = 75–100
if export_div > 0.6:  score = 55–74
if export_div > 0.4:  score = 30–54
if export_div > 0.2:  score = 10–29
else:                 score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
highly_diversified     → 75–100  (default 87)
moderately_diversified → 55–74   (default 65)
limited_diversification → 30–54  (default 42)
concentrated           → 10–29   (default 20)
highly_concentrated    → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Diversification indices are sensitive to the product classification granularity (HS2 vs HS6)
Re-export economies (e.g. transit hubs) may appear diversified without underlying productive capacity
Service exports are often excluded from standard merchandise trade diversification measures

economic/non_resource_export_share.label: Non-Resource Export Share
economic/non_resource_export_share.concept: The share of total merchandise exports that are non-extractive: all products excluding fuels (SITC 3) and non-ferrous metals / ores (SITC 27, 28, 68). A high non-resource export share indicates a diversified productive economy less dependent on commodity rents. Higher share = higher score (direct).
economic/non_resource_export_share.numerator: Total merchandise exports minus SITC 3 (fuels) and SITC 27+28+68 (mineral ores)
economic/non_resource_export_share.project_transform: Direct threshold scoring (higher non-resource share = higher score): > 80% → 80–100; > 60% → 60–79; > 40% → 35–59; > 20% → 10–34; ≤ 20% → 0–9.
economic/non_resource_export_share.score_formula:
# Raw input: non_res_export_pct — % exports that are non-fuel/non-mineral
# SITC classification: excludes groups 3 (fuels), 27+28+68 (minerals)
# Input transform: none (direct scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

if non_res_export_pct > 80:  score = 80–100
if non_res_export_pct > 60:  score = 60–79
if non_res_export_pct > 40:  score = 35–59
if non_res_export_pct > 20:  score = 10–34
else:                        score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
very_high_gt_80pct   → 80–100  (default 90)
high_60_80pct        → 60–79   (default 70)
moderate_40_60pct    → 35–59   (default 47)
low_20_40pct         → 10–34   (default 22)
very_low_lt_20pct    → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Countries with large re-export sectors may overstate non-resource share
Excludes service exports; for services-heavy economies the share is understated
Commodity price fluctuations cause denominator swings that alter the share even without structural change

international/sanctions.label: Sanctions
international/sanctions.concept: The degree to which a country faces international economic restrictions imposed by foreign governments or multilateral bodies. More severe sanctions reduce access to international financial systems, trade, and technology. The score is inverted: fewer/lighter sanctions = higher score.
international/sanctions.primary_source: US Treasury OFAC, EU External Action Service, UN Security Council
international/sanctions.series_id: Qualitative assessment of sanction regime type (no single global index)
international/sanctions.native_scale: Feature vocabulary from no_sanctions to total_isolation
international/sanctions.project_transform: Inverted feature vocabulary mapping (fewer sanctions = higher score): no_sanctions → 90–100; targeted_individuals_only → 70–89; sectoral_sanctions → 50–69; broad_economic_humanitarian_exceptions → 30–49; comprehensive_sanctions → 10–29; total_isolation → 0–9.
international/sanctions.score_formula:
# Raw input: qualitative assessment of sanction regime type
# No quantitative series; note: MORE/HEAVIER sanctions = LOWER score (inverted)
# Combination rule: qualitative_only

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (default scores):
no_sanctions                           → 90–100  (default 95)
targeted_individuals_only              → 70–89   (default 80)
sectoral_sanctions                     → 50–69   (default 60)
broad_economic_humanitarian_exceptions → 30–49   (default 40)
comprehensive_sanctions                → 10–29   (default 20)
total_isolation                        → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: No single global sanctions database covers all sanctioning entities (US OFAC, EU, UN, UK, Canada, Australia, individual country sanctions)
Targeted sanctions (on specific individuals or entities) and comprehensive sanctions have fundamentally different economic impacts; this indicator conflates them into a severity scale
UN Security Council sanctions (Chapter VII) are multilateral and legally binding; US/EU secondary sanctions affect non-sanctioned third parties extraterritorially
OFAC 50% Rule: any entity 50%+ owned by an SDN-listed party is itself treated as blocked, even if not explicitly named — the effective scope of US sanctions is broader than the named list

UNSCR 2664 (December 2022) created a standing humanitarian exemption across virtually all UN sanctions regimes, explicitly permitting delivery of humanitarian assistance without constituting a violation
Sanctions regimes evolve rapidly; the annual snapshot may miss intra-year changes
Formal sanctions and informal diplomatic/economic pressure (not coded as official sanctions) may have similar effects
Sanctions evasion via shadow fleets (Russia, Iran), front companies, and third-country procurement networks means actual economic restriction is often lower than the legal framework implies

secondary_sources.name: OFAC Sanctions Programs and Country Information

international/diplomatic_integration.label: Diplomatic Integration
international/diplomatic_integration.concept: The extent to which a country is recognized internationally, maintains active diplomatic relations, and participates in multilateral bodies. Assesses both formal recognition and substantive engagement in the international community.
international/diplomatic_integration.primary_source: News analysis, foreign ministry announcements, UN treaty body records
international/diplomatic_integration.native_scale: Feature vocabulary from full_relations to no_international_recognition
international/diplomatic_integration.project_transform: Feature vocabulary mapping: full_relations_active_international → 90–100; normal_minor_disputes → 70–89; partial_isolation_some_closures → 50–69; significant_isolation → 30–49; pariah_status → 10–29; no_international_recognition → 0–9.
international/diplomatic_integration.score_formula:
# Raw input: qualitative assessment of diplomatic status and international engagement
# Combination rule: qualitative_only

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (default scores):
full_relations_active_international → 90–100  (default 95)
normal_minor_disputes               → 70–89   (default 80)
partial_isolation_some_closures     → 50–69   (default 60)
significant_isolation               → 30–49   (default 40)
pariah_status                       → 10–29   (default 20)
no_international_recognition        → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: No standardized global index measures diplomatic integration; all values are qualitative estimates
Formal diplomatic recognition and substantive engagement often diverge (e.g., a country may maintain embassies while being effectively isolated)
UN membership and participation in specific treaty bodies provides objective anchors but does not capture the full picture
Regional blocs (AU, EU, ASEAN) may suspend or readmit members independently of universal recognition

secondary_sources.name: UN General Assembly — Member States and Voting Records
US State Department — Bilateral Relations Fact Sheets
CIA World Factbook — Government section (diplomatic relations)

international/foreign_military.label: Foreign Military
international/foreign_military.concept: The presence and nature of foreign military forces in the country. Used as a sovereignty indicator: no foreign presence signals full sovereignty; large uninvited foreign forces signal occupation or near-total loss of sovereignty. The score is inverted: less foreign military presence = higher score.
international/foreign_military.series_id: Qualitative assessment of foreign military presence type
international/foreign_military.native_scale: Feature vocabulary from no_foreign_presence to active_foreign_combat_operations
international/foreign_military.project_transform: Inverted feature vocabulary mapping (less foreign presence = higher score): no_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty → 90–100; limited_invited_advisors → 70–89; significant_invited_forces → 50–69; contested_mixed_presence → 30–49; occupied_major_uninvited → 10–29; active_foreign_combat_operations → 0–9.
international/foreign_military.score_formula:
# Raw input: qualitative assessment of foreign military presence type
# Note: MORE foreign presence = LOWER score (inverted — sovereignty indicator)
# Combination rule: qualitative_only

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (default scores):
no_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty → 90–100  (default 95)
limited_invited_advisors             → 70–89   (default 80)
significant_invited_forces           → 50–69   (default 60)
contested_mixed_presence             → 30–49   (default 40)
occupied_major_uninvited             → 10–29   (default 20)
active_foreign_combat_operations     → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Invited forces (with host government consent) and uninvited forces carry very different sovereignty implications but may receive similar scores
IISS Military Balance covers formal deployments; informal or covert forces are not captured
Peacekeeping missions (UN, AU, ECOWAS) raise different sovereignty questions than bilateral military deployments
Air operations and drone strikes may constitute foreign military activity without formal troop presence

secondary_sources.name: UN Peacekeeping — Troop and Police Contributors

international/fdi.label: FDI
international/fdi.concept: Net inflows of foreign direct investment as a percentage of GDP. FDI measures the acquisition of a lasting interest (≥10% ownership) by a foreign investor in a domestic enterprise. Used as an indicator of international business confidence in the country.
international/fdi.series_id: BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS (foreign direct investment, net inflows, % of GDP)
international/fdi.numerator: Net FDI inflows (gross inflows minus disinvestment, current USD)
international/fdi.native_scale: Can be negative (net outflows); typically −5% to +20% in practice
international/fdi.project_transform: Threshold scoring (higher FDI = higher score): ≥ 5% → 90–100; ≥ 2% → 70–89; ≥ 1% → 50–69; ≥ 0.5% → 30–49; ≥ 0% → 10–29; < 0% (net outflows) → 0–9.
international/fdi.score_formula:
# Raw input: fdi_pct_gdp — net FDI inflows as % of GDP (World Bank BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS)
# Definition: ≥10% ownership stake by foreign investor; net = inflows minus disinvestment
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if fdi_pct_gdp >= 5:    score = 90–100
if fdi_pct_gdp >= 2:    score = 70–89
if fdi_pct_gdp >= 1:    score = 50–69
if fdi_pct_gdp >= 0.5:  score = 30–49
if fdi_pct_gdp >= 0:    score = 10–29
else (negative):        score = 0–9   # net disinvestment

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
high_gt_5pct_gdp      → 90–100  (default 95)
moderate_2_5pct_gdp   → 70–89   (default 80)
low_1_2pct_gdp        → 50–69   (default 60)
very_low_05_1pct_gdp  → 30–49   (default 40)
minimal_lt_05pct_gdp  → 10–29   (default 20)
negative_net_outflows → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
international/fdi.geographic_coverage: Most countries (gaps for heavily sanctioned or conflict-affected states)
caveats: The 10% ownership threshold distinguishing FDI from portfolio investment is a definitional convention, not an economic reality threshold

Offshore financial centers (Luxembourg, Netherlands, Ireland) receive massive FDI inflows that are routing and holding structures, not real investment; some project countries may show inflated FDI for similar reasons
Round-tripping (domestic capital routed through a foreign intermediary and re-invested domestically) inflates FDI figures for some countries
A single large mining or oil concession can dominate FDI in small economies, making year-to-year figures volatile
Negative FDI (disinvestment exceeding new investment) appropriately scores near zero as it signals capital flight

secondary_sources.name: UNCTAD World Investment Report — Bilateral FDI Statistics

international/refugee_flows.label: Refugee Flows
international/refugee_flows.concept: Annual new displacement (refugees leaving the country + new internally displaced persons) as a proxy for crisis severity. Normalized by population. The score is inverted: more displacement = lower score.
international/refugee_flows.primary_source: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder + IDMC (Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre)
international/refugee_flows.series_id: UNHCR: new refugee outflows from country of origin (annual new displacement); IDMC: new internal displacements due to conflict. Metric: (new_refugees + new_IDPs) / population × 100.
international/refugee_flows.numerator: New refugee outflows + new internally displaced persons (annual flow)
international/refugee_flows.native_unit: Percent of population displaced per year
international/refugee_flows.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower displacement = higher score): ≤ 0 (net returns) → 90–100; < 0.1% → 70–89; < 1% → 50–69; < 3% → 30–49; < 10% → 10–29; ≥ 10% → 0–9.
international/refugee_flows.score_formula:
# Raw inputs: new_refugees (UNHCR annual new outflows), new_IDPs (IDMC conflict displacement),
#             population (World Bank SP.POP.TOTL)
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER displacement = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

displacement_pct = (new_refugees + new_IDPs) / population * 100

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if displacement_pct <= 0:    score = 90–100  # net returns
if displacement_pct < 0.1:   score = 70–89
if displacement_pct < 1:     score = 50–69
if displacement_pct < 3:     score = 30–49
if displacement_pct < 10:    score = 10–29
else:                        score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
net_returns_minimal_displacement  → 90–100  (default 95)
stable_minimal_new                → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_outflows_lt_1pct         → 50–69   (default 60)
significant_outflows_1_3pct       → 30–49   (default 40)
mass_displacement_3_10pct         → 10–29   (default 20)
catastrophic_gt_10pct             → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
international/refugee_flows.coverage_period: UNHCR: 1951–present; IDMC: 2003–present for conflict displacement; annual
international/refugee_flows.frequency: Annual (UNHCR updates quarterly; annual data used)
international/refugee_flows.methodology_break: UNHCR revised its methodology for counting refugees (especially Palestinian refugees and IDP counting conventions) at various points. IDMC began comprehensive conflict displacement tracking from 2003.
caveats: CRITICAL: UNHCR default view shows refugee STOCK (total registered); this project uses FLOW (new displacement per year) — stock and flow can diverge dramatically
IDPs are harder to count than international refugees; IDMC figures carry wider uncertainty, especially in active conflict zones
Registration gaps in conflict zones systematically undercount displacement (e.g., people fleeing to neighboring country without going through a UNHCR registration point)
Returns of refugees are a positive sign but can temporarily appear as 'negative displacement' in flow statistics
Displacement from natural disasters (not coded here) is tracked separately by IDMC and excluded from this indicator
Palestinian refugees are tracked under UNRWA, not UNHCR; covered if relevant countries are in scope

transparency/budget_transparency.label: Budget Transparency
transparency/budget_transparency.concept: Whether citizens and civil society can access comprehensive, timely government budget information across the full fiscal cycle — from pre-budget proposals through audit reports. Measured by the Open Budget Index.
transparency/budget_transparency.primary_source: Open Budget Index (OBI) — International Budget Partnership
transparency/budget_transparency.native_scale: 0–100 composite of 109 questions about public availability of eight budget documents (Pre-Budget Statement, Executive Budget Proposal, Enacted Budget, Citizens Budget, In-Year Reports, Mid-Year Review, Year-End Report, Audit Report), each question scored 0/33/67/100.
transparency/budget_transparency.score_formula:
# Raw input: OBI_score — Open Budget Index composite score (0–100, already on project scale)
# Type: direct — score passes through a threshold table but stays close to the OBI value
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Threshold scoring (direct; OBI score → project score range):
if OBI_score >= 80:  score = 80–100
if OBI_score >= 60:  score = 60–79
if OBI_score >= 40:  score = 40–59
if OBI_score >= 20:  score = 20–39
if OBI_score >= 1:   score = 1–19
else:                score = 0

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (used for non-survey years and countries not in OBI):
extensive_gt_80    → 80–100  (default 85)
substantial_60_80  → 60–79   (default 70)
limited_40_60      → 40–59   (default 50)
minimal_20_40      → 20–39   (default 30)
scant_lt_20        → 1–19    (default 10)
no_information     → 0       (default 0)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
Note: OBI published biennially; off-years interpolated linearly between survey rounds
transparency/budget_transparency.methodology_break: Coverage expanded from 85 countries (2006) to 125 (2023). Some score changes between rounds may reflect changes in researcher interpretation of methodology rather than actual transparency changes.
caveats: BIENNIAL FREQUENCY: data only available every two years; non-survey years require interpolation (linear or carry-forward), introducing uncertainty into annual trend lines
Measures formal availability of documents, not whether citizens can actually find, download, or understand them (bandwidth, language, web availability after survey date)
Assessment is done by local civil society researchers (not government self-reports), with IBP peer review — more reliable than self-reporting indices
A score of 61+ is IBP's threshold for 'sufficient' public budget information; below 61 indicates insufficient access
Not all 39 project countries are in every round; countries added later lack historical baselines

secondary_sources.name: PEFA (Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability) Assessments
IBP Open Budget Survey Data Portal

transparency/press_freedom.label: Press Freedom
transparency/press_freedom.concept: The degree to which journalists can investigate and report on government and other power without censorship, intimidation, or legal prosecution. Measured by the RSF World Press Freedom Index. The score is constructed so that higher values = better press freedom.
transparency/press_freedom.primary_source: RSF (Reporters Without Borders) World Press Freedom Index
transparency/press_freedom.series_id: RSF World Press Freedom Index — global score
transparency/press_freedom.native_scale: Post-2022: 0–100 where 100 = best press freedom; based on five equal-weight contextual indicators (political context, legal framework, economic context, sociocultural context, safety). Pre-2022: seven criteria, scale direction inverted (0 = best press freedom). Values in this dataset are normalized to 0 = worst, 100 = best.
transparency/press_freedom.project_transform: Post-2022 RSF: use directly (already 0=worst, 100=best). Pre-2022 RSF: apply 100 − RSF_raw to normalize direction. Both yield project score: 0–100 where 100 = best press freedom.
transparency/press_freedom.score_formula:
# Raw input: RSF_score — Reporters Without Borders World Press Freedom Index
# CRITICAL: scale direction changed in 2022
#   Pre-2022: 0–100 where 0 = best press freedom (inverted direction)
#   Post-2022: 0–100 where 100 = best press freedom (correct direction)
# Input transform: rsf_invert (applied pre-2022 only)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

if year < 2022:
    transformed = 100 - RSF_score  # invert pre-2022 direction: 0=best → 100=best
else:
    transformed = RSF_score         # post-2022 already: 100=best

# Threshold scoring after transform:
if transformed >= 85:  score = 85–100
if transformed >= 70:  score = 70–84
if transformed >= 55:  score = 55–69
if transformed >= 40:  score = 40–54
if transformed >= 15:  score = 15–39
else:                  score = 0–14

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
free_environment  → 85–100  (default 90)
satisfactory      → 70–84   (default 75)
problematic       → 55–69   (default 60)
difficult         → 40–54   (default 45)
very_serious      → 15–39   (default 25)
no_press_freedom  → 0–14    (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
transparency/press_freedom.methodology_break: CRITICAL: 2022 complete methodology redesign. Pre-2022: seven criteria, scale 0–100 (0=best); Post-2022: five indicators, scale 0–100 (100=best), new weighting structure. Scores before and after 2022 are NOT directly comparable. Rankings shifted substantially in 2022 due to measurement instrument change, not actual press freedom change.
caveats: METHODOLOGY BREAK IN 2022: pre-2022 and post-2022 scores use fundamentally different instruments and scale directions; trend analysis spanning 2021–2022 requires caution
Relies on expert network of press freedom specialists; RSF does not publicly disclose the full list of respondents
The safety indicator includes a quantitative abuses score (murders, detentions) that can cause volatility in small countries where one journalist killing has outsized impact
Pre-2022 methodology used the final score as the minimum of two sub-scores; this prevented high qualitative scores masking high abuses
Countries with limited international media coverage may have fewer RSF specialists, increasing per-respondent weight

secondary_sources.name: Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ) — Imprisoned and Killed Database
Freedom House — Freedom of the Press (archived 1980–2017)

transparency/statistical_transparency.label: Statistical Transparency
transparency/statistical_transparency.concept: Whether the government produces credible, timely, and accessible socioeconomic statistics. Encompasses the quality of the national statistical system, the methodologies used, and whether data is made publicly available.
transparency/statistical_transparency.series_id: SCI: IQ.SCI.OVRL (overall Statistical Capacity Indicator, 0–100). Post-2021: ODIN (Open Data Inventory) score or World Bank SPI as supplement.
transparency/statistical_transparency.native_scale: SCI (2004–2021): Three pillars — Methodology (25 criteria), Source Data (periodicity and coverage), Periodicity and Timeliness (publication frequency). Each pillar 0–100; composite = arithmetic mean. ODIN (2015/16–present): 22 categories × coverage + openness dimensions; 0–100 composite.
transparency/statistical_transparency.project_transform: Direct mapping (SCI or ODIN score = project score)
transparency/statistical_transparency.score_formula:
# Raw input: SCI_score — World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL, 0–100)
# Post-2021: ODIN (Open Data Inventory) score as supplement (also 0–100)
# Type: direct — both sources already on 0–100 scale
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Threshold scoring (direct):
if score >= 80:  score = 80–100
if score >= 60:  score = 60–79
if score >= 40:  score = 40–59
if score >= 20:  score = 20–39
if score >= 1:   score = 1–19
else:            score = 0

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
excellent_gt_80        → 80–100  (default 85)
good_60_80             → 60–79   (default 70)
moderate_40_60         → 40–59   (default 50)
poor_20_40             → 20–39   (default 30)
very_poor_lt_20        → 1–19    (default 10)
no_statistical_system  → 0       (default 0)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
Note: SCI discontinued 2021; ODIN (biennial) or World Bank SPI used as supplement from 2022
transparency/statistical_transparency.coverage_period: SCI: 2004–2021 (published annually for ~145 developing countries); ODIN: 2015/16–present (biennial)
transparency/statistical_transparency.geographic_coverage: SCI: ~145 developing countries (excludes high-income OECD); ODIN: ~185 countries
transparency/statistical_transparency.methodology_break: CRITICAL: World Bank discontinued the SCI in 2021 and replaced it with the Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI). SCI and SPI are NOT backward-compatible (r ≈ 0.77). ODIN (biennial) covers openness and digital availability, not methodology and capacity — a different concept. Series spanning 2021 should be treated as having a structural break.
caveats: STRUCTURAL BREAK AT 2021: SCI series ends; no backward-compatible replacement exists for pre-2021 coverage
SCI was designed for developing countries; high-income OECD countries are absent from the series
SCI measures statistical methodology, data sources, and publication timeliness — not whether data is accurate or open
ODIN measures openness and machine-readability of published data — a different concept from SCI's capacity focus
A country can have strong internal statistical capacity (high SCI) while keeping data behind paywalls (low ODIN)
For post-2021 years, ODIN biennial data must be interpolated; ODIN survey rounds do not align with OBI rounds

secondary_sources.name: World Bank Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI, from 2021)
UN Statistics Division — National Statistical Systems
PARIS21 — Partnership in Statistics for Development

transparency/legal_transparency.label: Legal Transparency
transparency/legal_transparency.concept: Whether laws, regulations, and government data are publicly accessible, and whether citizens have effective mechanisms to access government information. Measured by the World Justice Project's 'Open Government' factor of the Rule of Law Index.
transparency/legal_transparency.primary_source: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index
transparency/legal_transparency.series_id: WJP Rule of Law Index — Factor 3: Open Government
transparency/legal_transparency.native_scale: WJP Factor 3 covers four sub-factors: publicized laws and government data, right to information, civic participation, and complaint mechanisms. Each sub-factor is a weighted average of expert and household survey responses in ~120 questions. Composite Factor 3 scored 0–1.
transparency/legal_transparency.score_formula:
# Raw input: WJP_Factor3 — World Justice Project Rule of Law Index, Factor 3: Open Government (0–1)
# Sub-factors: publicized laws & data, right to information, civic participation, complaint mechanisms
# Input transform: wjp_multiply_100
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

transformed = WJP_Factor3 * 100  # → 0–100

# Threshold scoring after transform:
if transformed >= 80:  score = 90–100
if transformed >= 60:  score = 70–89
if transformed >= 40:  score = 50–69
if transformed >= 25:  score = 30–49
if transformed >= 10:  score = 10–29
else:                  score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
laws_published_searchable_foi    → 90–100  (default 95)
most_accessible_some_gaps        → 70–89   (default 80)
partial_significant_gaps         → 50–69   (default 60)
difficult_arbitrary_enforcement  → 30–49   (default 40)
minimal_rule_by_decree           → 10–29   (default 20)
no_accessible_framework          → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
transparency/legal_transparency.geographic_coverage: ~140 countries (expanding; not all 39 project countries covered every year)
caveats: WJP does not cover all 39 project countries: Syria, Libya are absent; Burkina Faso, CAR, DRC are intermittently absent; South Sudan has gaps
Gap years 2011–2012 require interpolation between 2010 and 2013 values; interpolated entries have lower reliability
First three editions (2008–2010) had limited country coverage and used somewhat different question sets
Expert surveys are supplemented by household surveys; both are perception-based, capturing what people experience, not objective legal architecture
Factor 3 (Open Government) is distinct from transparency in practice — a country may have formal right-to-information laws (high Factor 3) while routinely refusing FOI requests

The General Population Poll (GPP) is administered every few years per country, not annually; year-to-year changes between GPP waves primarily reflect changes in the annual Qualified Respondents Questionnaire (QRQ), not updated household experience data
Urban bias: the GPP polls only the three largest cities, systematically over-weighting urban populations; for predominantly rural countries this creates an upward bias

secondary_sources.name: US State Department Human Rights Reports — Access to Information

transparency/extractive_transparency.label: Extractive Transparency
transparency/extractive_transparency.concept: For countries with significant oil, gas, or mining sectors: whether revenues from natural resource extraction are publicly disclosed and independently reconciled. Based on EITI (Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative) membership and validation status. Not applicable for ~13 project countries with no significant extractive sector.
transparency/extractive_transparency.series_id: EITI country status (Compliant / Candidate / Suspended / Non-member)
transparency/extractive_transparency.native_scale: Categorical: Compliant (full validation) → Candidate (implementing, not yet validated) → Suspended (non-compliance) → Non-member / Not applicable (no extractive sector).
transparency/extractive_transparency.project_transform: Feature vocabulary mapping: eiti_compliant_satisfactory → 90–100; eiti_compliant_meaningful → 70–89; eiti_compliant_corrective_actions → 50–69; eiti_candidate_or_suspended → 30–49; no_membership_minimal_disclosure → 10–29; no_disclosure_opaque → 0–9.
transparency/extractive_transparency.score_formula:
# Raw input: EITI membership and validation status (assessed from eiti.org country profiles)
# No quantitative series
# NOT APPLICABLE for 13 project countries with no significant extractive sector
# Combination rule: qualitative_only

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (default scores):
eiti_compliant_satisfactory       → 90–100  (default 95)
eiti_compliant_meaningful         → 70–89   (default 80)
eiti_compliant_corrective_actions → 50–69   (default 60)
eiti_candidate_or_suspended       → 30–49   (default 40)
no_membership_minimal_disclosure  → 10–29   (default 20)
