no_disclosure_opaque              → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
Note: not_applicable countries are excluded from the Transparency dimension average
transparency/extractive_transparency.coverage_period: EITI founded 2003; country memberships vary by join date
transparency/extractive_transparency.frequency: Annual (based on EITI status at year-end)
transparency/extractive_transparency.geographic_coverage: EITI member/candidate countries only; non-members scored qualitatively
transparency/extractive_transparency.methodology_break: EITI revised its Standard in 2016 and 2023, changing what constitutes 'Compliant' status. Validation under different Standards is not directly comparable.
caveats: EITI is VOLUNTARY — countries with large extractive sectors and poor disclosure (e.g., Algeria, Libya, Syria) are non-members and scored accordingly
Afghanistan was delisted from EITI effective June 19, 2024 — Taliban governance prevented the multi-stakeholder group process from functioning; scores for 2024 onward should reflect non-member status
EITI status verifies that companies and governments DISCLOSED revenues; it does not verify the revenues disclosed are complete or accurate
EITI reports typically lag 2–3 years; the 2024 EITI report covers 2021–2022 data
Not applicable for 13 project countries: Tunisia, Egypt, Senegal, Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Croatia, Slovakia, Nepal, East Timor, Mexico, Peru, Armenia (no significant extractive sector or EITI excluded)
Compliant status sub-categories (satisfactory, meaningful, corrective actions required) reflect depth of implementation beyond basic compliance

secondary_sources.name: Natural Resource Governance Institute (NRGI) Resource Governance Index

transparency/control_of_corruption.label: Control of Corruption
transparency/control_of_corruption.concept: Perceptions of the extent to which public power is exercised for private gain, including both petty and grand forms of corruption, as well as 'state capture' — the influence of elites and private interests over government decisions. Higher values indicate less corruption.
transparency/control_of_corruption.native_scale: Standard normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; typical range −2.5 to +2.5. Aggregated from ~20 underlying sources including Transparency International, ICRG, and business surveys. Higher = less corruption.
transparency/control_of_corruption.score_formula:
# Raw input: WGI_CC — World Bank WGI Control of Corruption
# Series: CC.EST, standard normal distribution (z-score), typical range −2.5 to +2.5
# Higher values = less corruption = better
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: qualitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if WGI_CC >= 1.5:   score = 90–100
if WGI_CC >= 0.5:   score = 70–89
if WGI_CC >= -0.5:  score = 50–69
if WGI_CC >= -1.5:  score = 30–49
if WGI_CC >= -2.0:  score = 15–29
else:               score = 0–14

# Qualitative feature vocabulary (preferred):
minimal_corruption    → 90–100  (default 95)
low_corruption        → 70–89   (default 80)
moderate_corruption   → 50–69   (default 60)
elevated_corruption   → 30–49   (default 40)
high_corruption       → 15–29   (default 22)
pervasive_corruption  → 0–14    (default 7)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Corruption perception indices measure beliefs about corruption, not direct observation of corrupt acts; high-profile scandals can shift perceptions independently of underlying rates
WGI aggregates include Transparency International CPI, which is itself an aggregate of other surveys — creating overlap and potential double-counting
Standard errors are substantial (95% CI ~±0.5–1.0 units); changes smaller than 0.3 standard deviations should not be interpreted as real trends
State capture (elite corruption) and petty corruption have different implications for governance but are combined in this indicator

secondary_sources.name: ICRG Political Risk Rating — Corruption Component

population_mobility/net_migration.label: Net Migration
population_mobility/net_migration.concept: The net balance of emigration and immigration — whether more people are leaving or arriving. Interpreted as a signal of economic and political conditions: persistent net outflows indicate a country that people are fleeing; net inflows or balanced migration suggest normal or attractive conditions.
population_mobility/net_migration.primary_source: World Bank World Development Indicators / qualitative assessment
population_mobility/net_migration.series_id: SM.POP.NETM (net migration, 5-year estimates); qualitative assessment for annual values
population_mobility/net_migration.native_unit: Persons (net count); rate per 1,000 population used for thresholds
population_mobility/net_migration.native_scale: Net migration count; typically expressed as rate per 1,000 population. World Bank data is 5-year totals; annual rates are derived estimates.
population_mobility/net_migration.project_transform: Qualitative feature vocabulary (net migration rate per 1,000 population per year): strong_net_inflow (> +5) → 80–100; mild_net_inflow (+1 to +5) → 60–79; return_migration → 65–79; roughly_stable (−1 to +1) → 45–59; mild_net_outflow (−5 to −1) → 25–44; significant_net_outflow (−15 to −5) → 10–24; severe_net_outflow (< −15) → 0–9.
population_mobility/net_migration.score_formula:
# Raw input: qualitative assessment of net migration pattern
# Reference: World Bank SM.POP.NETM (5-year totals) used to calibrate annual assessments
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: qualitative_only (annual net migration rate unreliable cross-country)

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (defaults; rate = per 1,000 population per year):
strong_net_inflow       → 80–100  (default 90)   # > +5 per 1,000
mild_net_inflow         → 60–79   (default 70)   # +1 to +5 per 1,000
return_migration        → 65–79   (default 72)   # significant returnees post-conflict
roughly_stable          → 45–59   (default 52)   # −1 to +1 per 1,000
mild_net_outflow        → 25–44   (default 35)   # −5 to −1 per 1,000
significant_net_outflow → 10–24   (default 17)   # −15 to −5 per 1,000
severe_net_outflow      → 0–9     (default 5)    # below −15 per 1,000
Multi-feature rule: minimum
Note: context matters — economic emigration differs from crisis-driven flight;
small island states often show high emigration unrelated to political conditions
caveats: World Bank net migration data comes in 5-year intervals (e.g., 2015–2020); annual figures require interpolation or qualitative estimation
Net migration conflates very different phenomena: labor migration, refugee flight, retirement migration, and diaspora return
Undocumented migration (both emigration and immigration) is systematically under-counted in official statistics
Small countries can show extreme rates driven by a single large inflow/outflow event that are not indicative of general conditions
Return migration after conflict (a positive sign) can temporarily appear as near-zero net migration, masking the underlying improvement

population_mobility/remittances.label: Remittances
population_mobility/remittances.concept: Personal remittances received from abroad as a percentage of GDP. Remittances reflect diaspora engagement and can be a vital income source for fragile states. The score is non-monotonic: moderate remittances (2–5% of GDP) score highest, reflecting healthy diaspora ties without excessive dependence; very high remittances may indicate economic fragility.
population_mobility/remittances.series_id: BX.TRF.PWKR.DT.GD.ZS (personal remittances received, current USD, % of GDP)
population_mobility/remittances.numerator: Personal remittances received (includes workers' remittances + compensation of employees + migrant transfers)
population_mobility/remittances.native_scale: 0–50%+ (fragile small states can exceed 30%; most countries 0–5%)
population_mobility/remittances.project_transform: Non-monotonic qualitative scoring (moderate remittances score highest): minimal_lt_1pct → 45–60; low_1_2pct → 50–65; moderate_2_5pct → 55–70; high_5_10pct → 40–55; very_high_gt_10pct → 20–35.
population_mobility/remittances.score_formula:
# Raw input: remittances_pct_gdp — personal remittances received as % of GDP
# Source: World Bank BX.TRF.PWKR.DT.GD.ZS
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: qualitative_only
# NOTE: scoring is non-monotonic — moderate remittances score highest;
# very high remittances signal potential economic dependence and vulnerability

# Feature vocabulary → score ranges (defaults):
minimal_lt_1pct    → 45–60  (default 52)   # minimal diaspora engagement
low_1_2pct         → 50–65  (default 57)
moderate_2_5pct    → 55–70  (default 62)   # balanced engagement — highest score
high_5_10pct       → 40–55  (default 47)   # significant dependence risk
very_high_gt_10pct → 20–35  (default 27)   # high dependence vulnerability
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: World Bank BX.TRF.PWKR.DT.GD.ZS bundles workers' remittances, compensation of employees, and migrant transfers — three conceptually different flows
Informal remittance channels (hawala, hand-carried cash) are not captured in official statistics; true flows likely 20–50% higher in some corridors
Very high remittance ratios often reflect small economy size (denominator effect) rather than extremely large absolute flows
Remittance flows are countercyclical — they often increase during crises as diaspora supports family; this makes high remittances ambiguously positive or negative

secondary_sources.name: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics — Current Transfers

social/life_expectancy.label: Life Expectancy
social/life_expectancy.concept: Life expectancy at birth — the expected number of years a newborn would live given current age-specific mortality rates. A composite measure of health system capacity, nutrition, sanitation, and overall living conditions. Higher values reflect better outcomes.
social/life_expectancy.series_id: SP.DYN.LE00.IN (life expectancy at birth, total, years)
social/life_expectancy.score_formula:
# Raw input: life_exp_years — life expectancy at birth, total (years) (SP.DYN.LE00.IN)
# Definition: expected years of life given current age-specific mortality rates
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if life_exp_years >= 75:  score = 85–100
if life_exp_years >= 70:  score = 70–84
if life_exp_years >= 65:  score = 50–69
if life_exp_years >= 60:  score = 30–49
else:                     score = 0–29

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
high_gt_75     → 85–100  (default 92)
good_70_75     → 70–84   (default 77)
moderate_65_70 → 50–69   (default 60)
low_60_65      → 30–49   (default 40)
very_low_lt_60 → 0–29    (default 15)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
social/life_expectancy.coverage_period: 1960–present (World Bank); UN IGME revisions update historical series
caveats: Life expectancy at birth is a period measure (current mortality rates applied to a synthetic cohort), not a prediction of how long any individual will actually live
Conflict can cause sharp short-term declines that recover quickly once fighting stops; the series smooths over sub-annual spikes
HIV/AIDS caused major declines in Southern African life expectancy in the 1990s–2000s; recovery from AIDS-related mortality can mask persistent non-AIDS mortality problems

secondary_sources.name: UN IGME — Inter-agency Group for Child Mortality Estimation

social/infant_mortality.label: Infant Mortality
social/infant_mortality.concept: The probability of dying between birth and exactly one year of age, expressed per 1,000 live births. A sensitive indicator of child health, nutrition, access to clean water, and basic healthcare. The score is inverted: lower mortality earns a higher score.
social/infant_mortality.series_id: SP.DYN.IMRT.IN (mortality rate, infant, per 1,000 live births)
social/infant_mortality.project_transform: Inverted threshold scoring (lower mortality = higher score): < 10 → 80–100; < 25 → 55–79; < 50 → 30–54; < 80 → 10–29; ≥ 80 → 0–9.
social/infant_mortality.score_formula:
# Raw input: imr — infant mortality rate (deaths under age 1 per 1,000 live births)
# Source: World Bank SP.DYN.IMRT.IN (UN IGME estimates)
# Input transform: none
# Note: HIGHER IMR = LOWER score (inverted scale)
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds (inverted):
if imr < 10:   score = 80–100
if imr < 25:   score = 55–79
if imr < 50:   score = 30–54
if imr < 80:   score = 10–29
else:          score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
low_lt_10        → 80–100  (default 90)
moderate_10_25   → 55–79   (default 67)
elevated_25_50   → 30–54   (default 42)
high_50_80       → 10–29   (default 20)
very_high_gt_80  → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
social/infant_mortality.frequency: Annual (UN IGME estimates smoothed from survey data)
caveats: UN IGME estimates are modeled from household surveys (DHS, MICS), censuses, and vital registration; for countries with poor vital registration, estimates carry wide uncertainty
Conflict dramatically increases infant mortality through disruption of healthcare but this is hard to measure directly; survey-based estimates may lag actual mortality spikes by 3–5 years
Definition: deaths within first year of life per 1,000 live births; distinct from neonatal mortality (first 28 days) and under-5 mortality rate

secondary_sources.name: UN IGME — Levels and Trends in Child Mortality
UNICEF State of the World's Children — Statistics
WHO Global Health Observatory — Child Mortality

social/internet_users.label: Internet Users
social/internet_users.concept: The share of the population that has accessed the Internet from any device in the last three months. Reflects digital connectivity, access to information, and economic modernization. Higher values indicate greater connectivity.
social/internet_users.primary_source: World Bank World Development Indicators / ITU
social/internet_users.series_id: IT.NET.USER.ZS (individuals using the Internet, % of population)
social/internet_users.numerator: Persons who accessed the Internet in the last 3 months
social/internet_users.score_formula:
# Raw input: internet_pct — % of population using the Internet (IT.NET.USER.ZS)
# ITU definition: accessed the Internet from any location and device in the last 3 months
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if internet_pct >= 80:  score = 85–100
if internet_pct >= 60:  score = 70–84
if internet_pct >= 40:  score = 55–69
if internet_pct >= 20:  score = 35–54
if internet_pct >= 5:   score = 15–34
else:                   score = 0–14

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
very_high_gt_80pct → 85–100  (default 92)
high_60_80pct      → 70–84   (default 77)
moderate_40_60pct  → 55–69   (default 62)
low_20_40pct       → 35–54   (default 45)
very_low_5_20pct   → 15–34   (default 25)
minimal_lt_5pct    → 0–14    (default 7)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: ITU data relies on country self-reporting; many low-income countries lack regular ICT surveys and extrapolate from mobile subscription data
Access vs. use distinction: mobile data access (even minimal) counts as 'using the Internet'; does not distinguish meaningful connectivity from occasional access
Rural-urban digital divide can mean national averages mask near-zero rural connectivity alongside high urban penetration

secondary_sources.name: Our World in Data — Internet Access

social/health_expenditure.label: Health Expenditure
social/health_expenditure.concept: Current health expenditure as a percentage of GDP, covering all expenditures for health functions including public and private spending on hospital care, ambulatory care, medical goods, and public health programs. Higher spending (as % of GDP) indicates greater societal investment in health.
social/health_expenditure.primary_source: World Bank World Development Indicators / WHO
social/health_expenditure.numerator: Current health expenditure (public + private, current USD)
social/health_expenditure.score_formula:
# Raw input: health_exp_pct — current health expenditure as % of GDP (SH.XPD.CHEX.GD.ZS)
# WHO/World Bank definition: all recurrent and capital health spending; includes public + private
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if health_exp_pct >= 8:  score = 75–100
if health_exp_pct >= 5:  score = 50–74
if health_exp_pct >= 3:  score = 25–49
else:                    score = 0–24

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
high_gt_8pct      → 75–100  (default 87)
moderate_5_8pct   → 50–74   (default 62)
low_3_5pct        → 25–49   (default 37)
very_low_lt_3pct  → 0–24    (default 12)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: High health expenditure % of GDP does not guarantee good health outcomes; efficiency of spending varies enormously
Conflict disrupts health system financing and utilization; expenditure data may not reflect actual service availability
Private out-of-pocket payments are included; a high ratio driven by out-of-pocket costs (low public spending) may indicate financial hardship rather than good coverage
WHO data revisions are substantial; historical series are frequently revised as National Health Accounts are updated

social/education_expenditure.label: Education Expenditure
social/education_expenditure.concept: Government expenditure on education (all levels combined) as a percentage of GDP. Reflects public investment in human capital development. Higher values indicate a greater commitment of public resources to education.
social/education_expenditure.primary_source: World Bank World Development Indicators / UNESCO Institute for Statistics
social/education_expenditure.series_id: SE.XPD.TOTL.GD.ZS (government expenditure on education, total, % of GDP)
social/education_expenditure.numerator: General government expenditure on education (all levels, current USD)
social/education_expenditure.score_formula:
# Raw input: edu_exp_pct — government expenditure on education as % of GDP (SE.XPD.TOTL.GD.ZS)
# UNESCO/World Bank definition: general government expenditure on education, all levels
# Excludes household private education spending
# Input transform: none
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

# Quantitative thresholds:
if edu_exp_pct >= 6:  score = 70–100
if edu_exp_pct >= 4:  score = 45–69
if edu_exp_pct >= 2:  score = 20–44
else:                 score = 0–19

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
high_gt_6pct      → 70–100  (default 85)
moderate_4_6pct   → 45–69   (default 57)
low_2_4pct        → 20–44   (default 32)
very_low_lt_2pct  → 0–19    (default 10)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
caveats: Education expenditure data is among the most gap-prone in the WDI; conflict-affected countries may have no data for 5–10 year stretches
Excludes private household spending on education, which can be large in countries with weak public systems
High spending does not guarantee good education outcomes; enrollment rates, teacher quality, and curriculum matter equally
Military spending during conflict diverts from social spending; drops in education expenditure often coincide with conflict peaks

secondary_sources.name: UNESCO Institute for Statistics — Education Finance

social/hdi.label: Human Development Index
social/hdi.concept: The UNDP Human Development Index (HDI) is a composite measure of three dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life (life expectancy), access to knowledge (mean and expected years of schooling), and a decent standard of living (GNI per capita, PPP). It ranges from 0 to 1; higher values indicate higher human development. Scored on a 0–100 scale by multiplying by 100.
social/hdi.numerator: Geometric mean of health, education, and income dimension indices
social/hdi.native_scale: 0–1 where 1 = highest human development. UNDP categories: Very High ≥ 0.800; High 0.700–0.799; Medium 0.550–0.699; Low < 0.550.
social/hdi.project_transform: Multiply by 100 to convert to 0–100 scale, then threshold scoring: ≥ 80 → 80–100; ≥ 70 → 60–79; ≥ 55 → 35–59; ≥ 40 → 10–34; < 40 → 0–9.
social/hdi.score_formula:
# Raw input: hdi_value — UNDP Human Development Index (0–1 scale)
# UNDP definition: geometric mean of life expectancy, education, and income indices
# Input transform: multiply by 100 → 0–100 scale
# Combination rule: quantitative_preferred

transformed = hdi_value * 100
if transformed >= 80:  score = 80–100
if transformed >= 70:  score = 60–79
if transformed >= 55:  score = 35–59
if transformed >= 40:  score = 10–34
else:                  score = 0–9

# Qualitative feature vocabulary:
very_high_gt_80      → 80–100  (default 87)
high_70_80           → 60–79   (default 72)
medium_55_70         → 35–59   (default 50)
low_40_55            → 10–34   (default 22)
very_low_lt_40       → 0–9     (default 5)
Multi-feature rule: minimum
social/hdi.methodology_break: UNDP revised the HDI methodology in 2010 (shifted from arithmetic to geometric mean, updated goalposts) and again in 2021 (updated goalposts). Historical values before 2010 have been recalculated using the current methodology in the 2023 report; comparability across the full series is maintained by UNDP.
caveats: HDI does not capture inequality within countries; two countries with the same HDI can have very different distributions of wellbeing
UNDP publishes revised historical series with each report; scores from older reports are not comparable to the current series
The income component uses GNI per capita PPP, which undervalues subsistence economies and overstates income in high-cost urban economies
Education component uses expected years of schooling, which can remain high even when school quality collapses (e.g. conflict contexts)

secondary_sources.name: UNDP Global Data Lab HDI Data
Our World in Data — Human Development Index
